summary: Godot 4.5.1 on Windows — Crash when opening Color Picker in second instance (two simultaneous editor windows)

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: Godot Engine v4.5.1.stable.official.f62fdbde1
- Not tested in another versions

### System information

Godot v4.5.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 22621) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7680) - AMD Ryzen 7 1700X Eight-Core Processor (16 threads) - 31.91 GiB memory

### Issue description

Using Godot 4.5.1 on Windows. When two instances of the editor are running simultaneously, in the second instance only (the one opened after the first), opening the Color Picker dialog causes the editor to crash. The first instance remains unaffected. This suggests a problem specific to “multiple editor windows/instances + ColorPicker” scenario.

PS: There is no error logs being generated.

### Steps to reproduce

- On a Windows machine, launch the first instance of Godot 4.5.1 (Instance A).
- Without closing Instance A, launch a second instance of Godot 4.5.1 (Instance B).
- In Instance B (the second one), open any project (or test with a new/empty project).
- In Instance B, navigate to a context where the Color Picker dialog can be opened (for example: selecting a control theme color, or editing a Material property, etc).
- Click to open the Color Picker dialog: the editor (Instance B) crashes (closes unexpectedly and somethings  “Godot has stopped working” flahses). Instance A remains open and perfectly functional.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

The project just has a scene called Player with a ColorRect, the colorpick element clicked belongs to the ColorRect node.

summary: Correctly handle discarding of saved redo

description.text:
Fixes #112592

The bug is caused by time travelling shenanigans. When you undo after saving and then make another action, you end up in a different timeline and the saved version can no longer be reached. The editor did not recognize that, causing the inconsistency.

btw, 3.x also has this bug, but without error spam. The scene just appears as saved, while it has changes.

summary: Godot IDE's overly eager, unsaved shader rendering can cause system crash with non-terminating loops

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducable in 4.5.stable ### System information Godot v4.5.stable - macOS Sequoia (15.7.1) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Metal (Mobile) - integrated Apple M1 Max (Apple7) - Apple M1 Max (10 threads) - 64.00 GiB memory ### Issue description Godot compiles and renders shaders in the editor while they are being written; without waiting for the user to save or indicate they are finished working on it. In a gdshader, I briefly made a non-terminating `while` loop, when copying a code block and editing it to cover another case. The editor eagerly tried to render the shader, before I had finished making the new code valid, and this crashed my entire computer, (see screenshot below). I worked around the problem by using a different editor to fix the shader, to avoid Godot from eagerly rendering it and crashing again. (And replaced the `while` loop with a `for` loop to avoid a non-terminating loop.) ### Suggestions / ideas - Avoid rendering work-in-progress code before the user is ready, e.g. - Wait until the user saves before rendering - Have a "rebuild button" - Have a timout for shader execution in the editor, to catch infinite loops and prevent crashes ### Steps to reproduce I don't really want to reproduce this error and crash my computer again. But here is how I think it can be done: - Create a new project or open an existing one. - Create any node that can have a shader, like a Sprite2D or ColorRect. - Assign a new ShaderMaterial to it, and in the material, create a new Shader. - In the shader editor, paste the following: ```glsl shader_type canvas_item; void fragment() { float r = 1.0; while(true) { r *= 1.0; // just incase compile skips loop otherwise } COLOR.r = r; } ``` ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) (See above, I don't want to risk crashing my PC by making a reproducer. Not much is needed to reproduce.)

description.text: This allows users to dispatch these GitHub actions from their own repositories. Given that most users have automatic CI disabled, most probably won't make use of this. summary: Inconsistent Redo History

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in: 4.5.1 stable from Steam

### System information

Windows 10 Pro Versión	22H2 19045.6456 - v4.5.1.stable.steam [f62fdbde1] - 

### Issue description

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ccbedc04-a900-4a3e-a1c7-8cf605be7770

Undoing changes after saving a scene causes the "Inconsistent Redo History" error, which prevents saving the scene after undoing the saved changes. The workaround is to close Godot and reopen it, but all unsaved data will be lost.

### Steps to reproduce

Create a new project -> Add any node (I tested with Control and Node2D nodes) -> Save the scene -> Move the node more than twice (If you move it only once, the error doesn't appear) -> Save the scene -> Undo the changes with Ctrl + Z -> Move the node again -> Error

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

In theory, the error isn't specific to my project, it should be reproducible in any project.
But just in case, I'll leave a link to my project in case you can't reproduce it: https://github.com/QuaaDev/Proyecto-Wesnoth/tree/Inconsistent-Redo-History-Issue

summary: [Wayland] Adding a Popup to an editor plugin spams swap chain errors

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in v4.6.dev.custom_build [6fd949a6d]
- Reproducible in v4.5.2.rc.custom_build [6d411f0c0]

### System information

Godot v4.6.dev (41ca01d12) - Manjaro Linux #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Fri, 19 Sep 2025 16:09:36 +0000 on Wayland - Wayland display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 7900 XT (RADV NAVI31) - AMD Ryzen 9 7900X 12-Core Processor (24 threads) - 30.49 GiB memory

### Issue description

When trying to call the `popup()` method on a Popup as a child of a scene docked in the editor, the following errors appear:
```
  ERROR: platform/linuxbsd/wayland/display_server_wayland.cpp:767 - Condition "root_id == INVALID_WINDOW_ID" is true.
  ERROR: A swap chain was not created for the screen.
  ERROR: A swap chain was not created for the screen.
  ERROR: A swap chain was not created for the screen.
  [...]
  ERROR: platform/linuxbsd/wayland/display_server_wayland.cpp:863 - Condition "!windows.has(wd.root_id)" is true.
```
The popup itself never appears, and the swap chain error seems to be spammed once per frame until focusing another window (e.g. Alt+Tab).

The issue does not occur with the X11 version of the editor.

### Steps to reproduce

* Create a plugin, add a scene as a dock.
* Add a button to the docked scene that loads a popup scene.
* Click the button.

I will note that this only occurs when preloading the popup scene, not when creating one from scratch with `Popup.new()` (see commented code in MRP).

summary: Different behavior of @export custom Resources when reloading current scene and loading a new scene

description.text:
### Tested versions

Version: 4.5.1.stable

In my personal project the issue was present in older 4.x stable versions as well, however I was certain it was me doing something wrong. Since the bug was pretty minor to me, I just avoided using "reload_current_scene" until I came back to it to understand where my code is messing up.

### System information

Godot v4.5.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 Ti (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8108) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-11600K @ 3.90GHz (12 threads) - 15.87 GiB memory

### Issue description

When a custom resource type is created and saved as a file, you can load it in an exported field. The state of the resource on the disk isn't changed, but changes can be done in runtime (like in the project attached, a `count` field is incremented). Current scene is the only place where resource is used. When `get_tree().reload_current_scene()` is called, the state of the custom resources isn't reset to how it is on the disk. This is not the expected behavior (well, at least I expected). However, if you go to another scene and then reload the previous scene, the state of the resorce is reset - exactly like the expected behavior.
I believe that the final result of `reload_current_scene()` and loading current scene from a different one should be identical.

### Steps to reproduce

General steps: 
1. Create a custom resource.
2. Save it to disk.
3. Load in an exported field in current_scene.
4. Change the resource fields.
5. Call `get_tree().reload_current_scene()`.
6. Resource isn't reset.

For attached project:
1. Open the project
2. Click any of the "Count +1" buttons for how many times you like
3. Press "reload_current_scene()" button
4. Press "Go to Scene 2"
5. Press "Go to Scene 1"
6. Compare results

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[ResourceBug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23438999/ResourceBug.zip)

summary: Editor crash when deleting animation from SpriteFrames window after clearing resource without focus

description.text:
### Tested versions

**Versions Affected**: 4.4 ~ 4.5.1 (reproducible)
- v4.5.stable.mono.official [876b29033]
- v4.5.1.stable.official [f62fdbde1]

*Note: Due to network limitations, I was unable to test other versions. Apologies for the inconvenience.*

### System information

- **OS**: Windows 10 (build 19045)
- **Godot**: v4.5.stable.mono
- **Graphics**: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1050 Ti (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7652) - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility)
- **CPU**: 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13400F (16 threads)
- **RAM**: 15.83 GiB
- **Display**: Multi-window, 1 monitor

### Issue description

Hello Godot team,

I'm a Godot 4 developer from China. While working on my project, I encountered a critical issue related to the AnimatedSprite2D node where certain operations on the `sprite_frames` property cause the editor to crash.

**Expected Behavior**

After clearing the `sprite_frames` resource, the SpriteFrames editor window should either:
- Close automatically, or
- Clear its content and display an appropriate message about the missing resource

**Actual Behavior**

The SpriteFrames window remains open after resource deletion, and any interaction with it causes an editor crash.

**Additional Notes**
- I searched using relevant keywords but didn't find similar reported issues
- This is 100% reproducible following the steps above
- Minimal reproduction project: Not applicable (reproducible in any project)

### Steps to reproduce

1. Create an `AnimatedSprite2D` node
2. Create a new `SpriteFrames` resource for the `sprite_frames` property and click to edit it (this opens the SpriteFrames editor window)
3. *(Optional)* Perform some operations in the SpriteFrames window (adding animations, setting frames) - these don't affect the outcome
4. At this point, focus is on the `sprite_frames` property (indicated by blue highlighting). To reproduce the issue, we need to remove this focus
5. Click on any other node or area in the screen to make the AnimatedSprite2D node lose focus (the SpriteFrames window will close automatically)
6. Now click again on the AnimatedSprite2D node - the SpriteFrames window reopens, but notice that the `sprite_frames` property no longer has focus (black text, not blue)
7. Right-click the `sprite_frames` property and select "Clear" - the SpriteFrames resource is deleted (if you had set default animation frames, the 2D view will update correctly), but the SpriteFrames window remains open with unchanged content
8. Finally, click either the "Delete Animation" button or the "New Animation" button in the SpriteFrames window - the Godot editor crashes immediately

**Key Observation**

The crash occurs specifically when clearing the `sprite_frames` property while it doesn't have focus. If the property has focus when cleared, the SpriteFrames window closes properly and no crash occurs.

summary: The quick open dialog interaction feel should be harmonized (mouse pointer, button styles among others)

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.6-dev3 ### System information Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS ### Issue description The quick open script dialog's interaction feels a little off in comparison to the rest of Godot and desktop applications in general. Every interactive element shows a pointer cursor, which is more a web thing; less a desktop application standard. IMHO the pointer cursor should be sparingly used, only for hypertext links or things that have a really low affordance. Interactive elements should use the normal arrow cursor instead, like the rest of Godot buttons and toolbars. The button to toggle between the list and grid mode does not have the hover highlight rectangle like other toolbar buttons. In addition, by having a look at the source code, the measurements of this dialog seem not to take into account the spacing settings (compact vs spacious or custom values) set by the user or the theme. Everything is hardcoded. ### Steps to reproduce Create a blank project. Create a couple empty gdscript files. Create a new 2d scene. From the inspector, at the very bottom, click the folder button next to the script entry. The dialog opens and you can interact with it, seeing the things I mentioned. ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

summary: The revert button in inspector and settings items triggers when the mouse button is pressed down instead of released

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.6-dev3 - Reproducible in 4.4 ### System information Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS ### Issue description In the inspector dock and settings dialogs, each row has a "revert" button that shows up when the value is different than the default setting. Clicking this button reverts the setting to the default value. What seems surprising is that the button triggers as soon as the mouse button is pressed down i.e. the "mousedown" event. It should trigger when the click is released only, like regular buttons i.e. the "mouseup". This is expected for buttons that have one discrete action like this one. This action is destructive - you can lose a custom value with no "cancel" action to get it back. The standard trigger-on-release approach of mouse-operated buttons gives a chance to cancel by dragging away from the button while pressed before releasing. ### Steps to reproduce Start a new project. Create a scene, and change a setting in the inspector. The revert button should appear. Click on the revert button with the mouse button specifically and hold it down. If using the laptop trackpad, *do not tap it to click*; use the physical buttons if present or connect a USB mouse. Notice that the setting is already reverted, as you are still holding the mouse button down. ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

summary: Add error message to Quick Open dialog if callback is invalid

description.text:
Helps users diagnose and work around (but does not *fix*) issues like #112503.

Due to the way other parts of Godot are built, the Instant Preview function for the Quick Open dialog sometimes receives a callback that is invalid by the time it's ready to use it. This PR adds an error message explaining when this happens, and suggests to the user that they disable Instant Preview if it's currently enabled.

Following #112547, hopefully the conditions that cause this error shouldn't come up. But if they do, it will be very helpful to notify the users/developers rather than just silently failing.

summary: Windows: Fix window_get_size_with_decorations returning an invalid size when restoring from minimize

description.text:
Fixes #79754.

In Windows (replicated in Windows 10 & 11), minimizing then restoring a window that has both a `size` and a `max_size` set will cause the window to shrink to the smallest size window possible.

This PR fixes it by storing the width and height from `GetWindowRect()` and reading from that if the window is minimized. This is the exact same way we already solve the issue when reading from `GetClientRect()` in `window_get_size`.

summary: Boolean Instance Shader Parameters Cross-Conflicting with Multiple Materials on Same Mesh with Visual Shaders

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproduceable in v4.5.1.stable.steam [f62fdbde1]

### System information

Windows 11 - Vulkan (Forward+) - NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1070 - AMD Ryzen 9 5900X 12-Core Processor (24 threads) - 32 GB RAM

### Issue description

I've run into a very peculiar but consistently reproduceable bug which is a little tricky to explain. Normally, when a mesh has multiple material slots, and you apply different materials to those slots which have shader parameters set to the "Instance" qualifier, those parameters get portioned and separated out as best as possible, with an upper limit as to how many can be processed, as well as linking parameters with the same names together. This process is working properly for all parameter types... EXCEPT booleans.

If multiple materials define different boolean shader values with the "Instance" qualifier set, something very screwy is going on which is causing them to get cross-connected behind the scenes, leading to whatever states they are supposed to represent being wrong when a scene is loaded/instanced, and leading to unexpected behaviour when one of the values is altered.

Since this is difficult to explain I recommend loading up the MRP I have provided. This project has a simple mesh in place which has two material slots, both of which have been given unique materials, both materials having unique visual shaders, with one shader having a "BlueWhenTrue" Instance parameter, the other shader having a "RedWhenTrue" Instance parameter, yet when you first load the project, despite only ONE of those booleans being marked true, the mesh will be behaving as if BOTH are marked true, and if you turn one on, both colours show up, and if you turn one off, both colours disappear!

I can also confirm this happens regardless of what material slots you are using, be it in the mesh resource or the surface or material overrides. I am uncertain if this happens with code-based shaders as I work exclusively with visual shaders, but because this is happening outside of the shader code I suspect it would.

### Steps to reproduce

To reproduce this problem you need the following things:
* A Mesh resource which has more than one material slot
* Different materials and shaders applied to at least two slots
* Differently named boolean parameters qualified as "Instance" in each material's visual shader

Then, all you do is reload the scene and you will find adjusting the boolean Instance Shader Parameters for the mesh will not be working as intended.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[mrp_for_boolean_instance_shader_bug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23428037/mrp_for_boolean_instance_shader_bug.zip)

```
extends Control

var mouse_over = false
var time_passed = 0.
@onready var label = %Label

func _ready():
	mouse_entered.connect(_on_mouse_entered)
	mouse_exited.connect(_on_mouse_exited)
	
func _process(delta):
	time_passed += delta
	position.x = sin(time_passed) * 100. + 500.
	label.text = "mouse over" if mouse_over else "mouse not over"
	label.modulate = Color.GREEN if mouse_over else Color.RED
	
func _on_mouse_entered():
	print_debug("Mouse entered control node")
	mouse_over = true
	
func _on_mouse_exited():
	print_debug("Mouse exited control node")
	mouse_over = false
```

summary: Fix `Control::_update_minimum_size()` not updating offsets

description.text:
* Closes #100536 
* Does not reintroduce RTL bug as seen in #106477

Before this PR, whenever `Control::set_position` or `Control::set_size` was called, they would "freeze" the container's minimum size by fixing its offsets. If the Container was made smaller by resizing/deleting a child node (e.g., an `HBoxContainer` with a child deleted) after `set_position` or `set_size` had been called, it would not shrink to match the new expected size. 

This PR introduces an offset recalculation in `Control::_update_minimum_size` before `_size_changed()` is called, which fixes the issue. 

Before PR

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4f9bcaae-690a-46f9-94d9-a1a41a394654

After PR

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/00f1d6f2-4fe8-4477-8faa-2a7bc006a813

*Note: I don't know why the recordings are so messed up but they show the behavior and fix, so good enough for me*

description.text:

### Issue description

Comments are successfully stripped when exporting a release/production build of a game including single and multi line comments in GDScript `.gd`. However, the stripping process leaves behind empty lines where the comments were and these are not removed. Newlines that the user creator/user/programmer put there, are left too, but that's debatable.

It also seems like specifically multiline comments `"""` are not stripped at all inside GDScript functions (they are outside of functions).

In addition, shader files `.gdshader` do not strip any comments at all, no matter what, despite there being even greater potential to decrease export size, since the shader language syntax doesn't require newlines at all.

### Steps to reproduce

- Export the game using a release template without debug.
- Use GDRE for example to "decompile" the game file and look at the source files.

summary: LSP: Add stdio mode

description.text:
Closes godotengine/godot-proposals#464

Now Godot can be started as a headless LSP server that reads from stdin and writes to stdout with the `--lsp` CLI parameter.

Bellow is an example of [`helix`](https://helix-editor.com/) using the stdio LSP

![Helix_Godot_LSP_stdio](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bcd7dac7-eb3b-4b2b-847c-bbf226d0ea41)

## Helix configuration example

`helix` configuration to get it working `helix/languages.toml`
```toml
[language-server.godot]
command = "<YOUR GODOT BINARY FULL PATH>"
args = [ "--lsp" ]
```

## Testing tool

In order to make testing this easier and be sure that having multiple instances of Godot running doesn't broke the project and the LSP works I created a stress testing and validation tool.
It's spawns multiple concurrent LSP servers and continuously performs various checks to ensure they correctly handles file changes, symbol tracking, and diagnostics.
The tool does for each LSP server that spawns runs some tests in a forever loop. To prevent issues and because a regular Godot user won't edit code in two different editors at the same time,
the checks are executed for only one LSP server at a time.
Currently the checks are (open to write more if makes sense):
- Symbol tracking validation: Adds random functions to GDScript files and verifies the LSP recognizes them
- Diagnostics validation: Creates parent-child class relationships, removes the parent class, and confirms the LSP reports the expected diagnostic error ("Could not find base class")
I'm uploading binaries of the tool for Windows, Linux and Mac (I only tested it on Mac, so let me know if you are facing some issue)

Before start testing I encourage you to open Godot with the project that you want to test and if it's possible also have open an external editor that connects to Godot LSP Server through TCP (e.g. VSCode)
To verify that the use of a lot of LSP Servers didn't broke the project you could do a git diff on it or even export it an run it.

### Usage Example
We will use Godot 2D platformer demo:
```bash
# Clone the demo project
git clone https://github.com/godotengine/godot-demo-projects
cd godot-demo-projects/2d/platformer

# Run the test with 3 clients (default)
godot-lsp-test-os-arch -godot /path/to/godot -project .

# Run with 5 clients, checking every 10 seconds, with verbose logging
godot-lsp-test-os-arch -godot /path/to/godot -project . -clients 5 -interval 10 -verbose
```

### Binaries (Windows, Linux and Mac)
[godot-lsp-test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23552911/godot-lsp-test.zip)

summary: DisplayServerX11: Missing mouse motion events when `Input.use_accumulated_input` is false

description.text:
### Tested versions

reproducible in: v4.5.1.stable.official [f62fdbde1]
ive already identified the code that causes this and it's been there since the beginning, (aka affects all versions in theory)

### System information

Godot v4.5.1.stable - Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie) 13 on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated Intel(R) HD Graphics 620 (KBL GT2) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-7200U CPU @ 2.50GHz (4 threads) - 15.49 GiB memory

### Issue description

when `Input.use_accumulated_input` is false (and also when it is turned on), many mouse motion inputs are dropped. on my slow laptop only ~25 of 100 inputs are parsed when running this line of bash:
`for i in $(seq 100 200); do xdotool mousemove $i 200; done; xdotool mousedown 4 mouseup 4`
(i have a tester project that goes with this line of bash)

in general this is not that big of a deal as this affects not many games (`Input.use_accumulated_input` off and `MOUSE_MODE_CAPTURED` on).

to be clear, what happens is that every time inputs from your actual mouse are processed (usually once per frame) instead of processing all motion inputs, **it accidentally skips all consecutive motion inputs except the last**

strangely this bug does NOT happen when `MOUSE_MODE_CAPTURED` is used (makes sense if u see the code)
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/cb3af5afff15669a684ba4f04b9aa5c70dfd09ad/platform/linuxbsd/x11/display_server_x11.cpp#L5294-L5312

i thinks the first `break` was originally supposed to break out of the switch, but now it doesnt

### Steps to reproduce

run the mrp, put window in the top left corner and run this command a few times without focusing on the window (the first time will be junk bc of the extra mouse inputs)

`for i in $(seq 100 200); do xdotool mousemove $i 200; done; xdotool mousedown 4 mouseup 4`

i get this on x11:
```
event_count: 4 | accumulated_input = false
event_count: 5 | accumulated_input = true
event_count: 4 | accumulated_input = false
event_count: 5 | accumulated_input = true
```
it should definitely say 100 (or 101) when accumulated_input is false

summary: VehicleWheel3D suspension force calculation is wrong, and N/mm unit is misleading

description.text: ### Tested versions Godot v4.5.stable.mono ### System information Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8157) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-11800H @ 2.30GHz (16 threads) - 15.75 GiB memory ### Issue description Currently, the stiffness of the VehicleWheel3D node is stored as N/mm unit, however inside [vehicle_body_3d.cpp](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/cb3af5afff15669a684ba4f04b9aa5c70dfd09ad/scene/3d/physics/vehicle_body_3d.cpp#L559), this value is multiplied by spring offset (unit meters) without any unit conversion. Then later, the force value is multiplied by the vehicle mass, which makes no sense. When people use the vehiclewheel3d and see the stiffness as a unit of Newtons per length (mm), they expect it to follow Hooke's Law, which is F=kx, where k is stiffness in terms of N/m, and x is the spring offset in terms of m. There should be no vehicle mass in this equation My proposed change is to remove the mass multiplication, and add "*1000.0" into the formula as a conversion from N/mm to N/m. This would accurately reflect the unit N/mm displayed to the users. Pseudocode: ``` force = wheel_info.m_suspensionStiffness * 1000.0 * length_diff * wheel_info.m_clippedInvContactDotSuspension; ... // RESULT wheel_info.m_wheelsSuspensionForce = force; ``` The suspension length is also calculated wrong, which is solved by this PR [112440](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/112440) ### Steps to reproduce 1. Create a new 3D project in Godot v4.5.stable.mono. 2. Add a VehicleBody3D with mass 400kg node to the scene. (so weight is 400*9.8=3920N). 3. Add 4 VehicleWheel3D node as a child of the VehicleBody3D. (so we expect each wheel holds 3920/4=980N). Use stiffness=16N/mm; suspension_travel=spring_rest_length=0.5m, wheel_radius=0.5m 5. While the project is running, once the vehicle is stable, change the mass of the vehicle up and down. Notice how the vehicle does not move. This indicate the force strength increase or decrease depends on the chassis mass which does not reflect the N/mm unit. 6. For the same suspension properties, a heavier vehicle is expected to compress more than a lighter vehicle, but it is not the case for the current implementation. ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

summary: Array @export variables do not save when populated through a @tool script if they were previously empty

description.text:
### Tested versions

v4.5.stable

### System information

Windows 11, Godot v4.5.stable (this is a code error, unlikely to be hardware related)

### Issue description

When populating an empty array using a ```@tool``` script, the array will not save and will be empty again after reloading the project.

- This only affects empty arrays. Adding new items to an array that already contains items works fine.
- Editing the array manually in the Inspector _after_ populating it through a tool script fixes it, but having to do that every time is not always a feasible workaround.
- Creating an new array with ```array_name = []``` and assigning it does not fix the issue, I've checked.

### Steps to reproduce

- Create a test node with an ```@export``` variable of type ```Array```. It makes no difference whether the array is statically typed or not.
- Add the test node to the scene tree. Do not edit the array in the inspector, as the bug only affects empty arrays.
- Create a ```@tool``` script that adds a random item (or multiple) the array. This can be anything, but for simplicity, the easiest type of script to create would be a button using ```@export_tool_button```.
- Run the tool script (if using ```@export_tool_button```, add the node containing the tool script to the scene and click the button).
- Select the node containing the array. You'll notice the array now contains the values the script added. Do **not** edit the array as this negates the issue.
- Save the project.
- Close and reopen the project.
- The array has gone back to being empty.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[array-bug-mrp.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23406584/array-bug-mrp.zip)

To see for yourself, navigate to "Tool node" in test_scene.tscn and click the "Populate Array" button in the Inspector. Then, navigate to "Node with array" to see the added items. Save and reload **without editing the array manually** and it will be empty again.

### Issue description

Hi everyone, I am currently trying to get [J-Dax's Godot 4.3 C++ code for the Your First 2D Game Tutorial](https://github.com/j-dax/gd-cpp/tree/main) to work within Version 4.5 of Godot. (My project code [can be found here](https://github.com/kburchfiel/cpp_yf2dg/tree/main). I compiled it with [Version 4.5 of the godot-cpp code](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-cpp/tree/4.5).) I'd eventually like to use his code to create 4.5-compatible C++ snippets that will be equivalent to the GDScript and C# snippets in the existing documentation.

So far, I've added in some C++ code for the [Coding the Player](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/4.5/getting_started/first_2d_game/03.coding_the_player.html) scene. I'm able to see the new Player class that was created within my GDExtension script; however, when I attempt to add this class to my project, Godot crashes with the following error:

`ERROR: Node not found: "AnimatedSprite2D" (relative to "/root/@EditorNode@20438/@Panel@14/@VBoxContainer@15/DockHSplitLeftL/DockHSplitLeftR/DockHSplitMain/@VBoxContainer@26/DockVSplitCenter/@VSplitContainer@62/@VBoxContainer@63/@EditorMainScreen@103/MainScreen/@CanvasItemEditor@10871/@VSplitContainer@10516/@HSplitContainer@10518/@HSplitContainer@10520/@Control@10521/@SubViewportContainer@10522/@SubViewport@10523/Player/Player").`

Interestingly, when I downloaded, compiled, and launched J-Dax's own 4.3 project, a similar error occurred when I tried to add *his* Player to the scene--even though the game itself ran fine.

I did *not* encounter this issue with the ['Getting started' GDExtension example](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/4.5/tutorials/scripting/cpp/gdextension_cpp_example.html); I was able to add a GDExample node to my scene for that project without any trouble.

I imagine I might be overlooking something obvious. Any help with debugging this would be greatly appreciated! If it's relevant, I'm using Godot 4.5 with Linux Mint and an x86-64 processor.
